system_description.overview: Lumari is an AI-first platform that helps teams use AI to solve the most pressing company needs. The platform provides automated AI management, integrations with existing systems (CRMs, Slack, documents, databases), and compliance support services.

products.AI Agent: Custom, autonomous tools and workflows leveraging LLMs, AI, and agentic flows. Supports a wide range of AI-powered use cases across industries with built-in integration capabilities for third-party systems and tools.
products.Context-Aware Agents: Agents that adapt and improve by learning from company workflows and data context.
products.Instant Deployment: Launch fully functional internal apps and agentic workflows in minutes without backlog or ticketing delays.
products.Compliance Support Services: Comprehensive white-glove onboarding, setup, framework implementation, and ongoing expert guidance.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: Supabase (carve-out subservice organization for data center services)
infrastructure.physical_presence: None - no physical offices or servers; fully cloud-based
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (production servers at Supabase maintain failover capabilities hosted in high availability data centers with multiple availability zones)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: System hosted in Supabase VPC environment with segmented VPCs and access control lists (ACLs). Network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality.
network_architecture.api_gateway: Application gateway with firewalls configured on it
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS employed via Supabase in conjunction with security policy network settings. Intrusion detection system utilized to monitor and detect unauthorized access or anomalies within the network.
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access done through a bastion host with a virtual private network (VPN)
network_architecture.tls: TLS using certificates from established third-party certificate authority for web-based systems
network_architecture.content_filtering: Systems restricted to internet with content filtering system routed through proxy server
network_architecture.topology: User requests -> TLS encryption -> Backend API (Service Boxes) -> Supabase (database) + AI Backend + Worker Service. Deployment via Vercel and GitHub. Monitoring via Sentry. AI via Google Gemini. Management via Google Workspace and Linear.

application_architecture.pattern: Hybrid (Backend API + AI Backend + Worker Service as separate service boxes)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test and production environments (changes developed and tested in separate environment before implementation - CC8.1.4)

authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform, for all remote access, and for source code tool access
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination of employment
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Controlled through Supabase password policy with periodic forced changes, password expiry and complexity requirements. Accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts.

encryption.in_transit: TLS (version not specified) using certificates from established third-party CA
encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest
encryption.key_management: Cloud provider key management service to store and manage encryption keys. Access to production environment access keys restricted to authorized individuals.

ci_cd_devops.ci_cd_tool: GitHub (inferred from network diagram showing GitHub under Deployment Services)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Repository branch rules configured to ensure every merge request to move change to production environment requires additional approval. Developers do not make changes to application code in production without additional approval.
ci_cd_devops.iac: Infrastructure as code / configuration management tool used for baseline configurations and rollback capability (tool not named)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management set up by CTO with monthly patch management reviews. Workstations configured for automatic updates and scanned daily for patch compliance. Patches tested for stability/availability before production deployment.

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Sentry (error/performance monitoring, visible in network diagram)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled to monitor administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions at application and infrastructure level, changes to functions, security configurations, permissions, and roles
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events. Monitoring tool generates alerts when specific predefined thresholds are met.

third_party_services.purpose: Primary data center and database services (subservice organization)
Source code management and deployment services

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed and configured on all production servers and Mac-based workstations/laptops. Auto-scan on continuous basis, definitions updated weekly for production servers.
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications, with scan frequency adjusted as required. Performed by internal IT Infrastructure team.
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party. Results reviewed by IT management. High/critical findings remediated in timely manner.

untestable: CC2.2.3 - Significant changes to people, roles and responsibilities could not be tested because there were no significant changes made during the engagement period
CC3.2.1 - Cybersecurity insurance could not be tested because there were no cyber incidents reported during the engagement period
CC6.5.2 - Customer data deletion upon termination could not be tested because there were no customer terminations during the engagement period
CC7.3.1 - Incident response policies could not be tested because there were no security incidents reported during the engagement period
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 - Physical access to facilities: Supabase responsible for data center physical security, not in scope
A1.2 - Environmental protections: Lumari has no office/working space; managed by building management/Supabase
Processing Integrity - Determined not relevant to the system
Privacy - Determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC8.1.2 - Branch protection rules requiring approval for all production merges
CC8.1.3 - Source code changes logged, time-stamped, attributed to author with MFA-protected access
CC6.1.1 - MFA enforced for all administrative and remote access
CC7.4.1 - BCDR plans documented, reviewed annually, and tested with different loss scenarios
CC7.4.2 - Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration and alerting for backup failures
CC6.6.1 - Firewalls and IDS configured on application gateway to prevent unauthorized access
CC4.1.1 - Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party
CC9.2.1 - Vendor management with annual SOC report reviews and quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports

infrastructure.rationale: Relies entirely on Supabase as subservice organization (carve-out). Multi-AZ via Supabase but no multi-region. No named IaC tool. No CDN mentioned. Render listed as infra provider in metadata but architecture diagram centers on Supabase. Very thin infrastructure disclosure - heavy vendor dependency with limited direct control.

application_architecture.rationale: Network diagram shows Backend API + AI Backend + Worker Service pattern suggesting some service separation, but no languages, frameworks, or API style disclosed. Vercel for deployment suggests a modern JS/TS stack but this is inference. Separate environments confirmed. Branch protection and code review in place.

data_layer.rationale: Single database provider (Supabase/PostgreSQL). No caching layer, data warehouse, or file storage mentioned. Daily backups with multi-AZ confirmed. Data classification and retention policies exist. Per-tenant segregation confirmed (CC6.1.3). Missing specifics on backup encryption, retention periods, and point-in-time recovery.

security_posture.rationale: Good coverage: MFA enforced, IDS/IPS via Supabase, annual pen testing, monthly vulnerability scans, endpoint protection, server hardening, cybersecurity insurance. Firewalls on application gateway. However, no WAF mentioned, no named SIEM, and vulnerability scanning is internal only. Content filtering through proxy server is a positive.

devops_maturity.rationale: GitHub for source control with branch protection rules requiring approval. IaC/configuration management tool used but not named. Separate environments confirmed. Emergency change process documented. Monthly patch reviews. No named CI/CD pipeline tool, no container orchestration mentioned.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy exists, documented and reviewed annually. Annual testing of DR plans with different loss scenarios. Daily backups with multi-AZ. Multi-AZ failover via Supabase. However, no RTO/RPO disclosed, no multi-region failover, and DR capability is heavily dependent on Supabase subservice organization.

vendor_diversity.rationale: 8 vendors identified (Supabase, Vercel, GitHub, Sentry, Google Gemini, Google Workspace, Linear, Render). However, critical single-vendor dependency on Supabase for all database and data center services. Google Gemini as sole AI provider is another concentration risk. Vendor management program is solid with annual SOC reviews.

overall.rationale: Lumari presents a reasonably mature security posture for an early-stage AI company with a clean (unqualified, zero exceptions) SOC 2 Type 2 report. However, the report is notably light on technical specifics - no programming languages, frameworks, specific database configurations, or detailed architecture disclosed. Heavy reliance on Supabase as carve-out subservice organization means much of the infrastructure security is delegated. The 3-month audit period (May-Aug 2025) is notably short. Strong on process controls and governance for company size.

red_flags: Very short audit period of only 3 months (May 15 - Aug 15, 2025) - minimum viable for Type 2
Heavy single-vendor dependency on Supabase for all data center and database services with carve-out method
No specific programming languages, frameworks, or database configurations disclosed anywhere in the report
No WAF (Web Application Firewall) mentioned despite being a web-based AI platform
Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria excluded as 'not relevant' despite handling customer data through AI agents
Extremely small org structure (only CEO and CTO shown in org chart) raises questions about segregation of duties
yellow_flags: No named SIEM tool for security event correlation and analysis
No multi-region failover capability - all dependent on Supabase availability zones
No RTO/RPO explicitly stated in the report
IaC/configuration management tool used but not named - unclear maturity level
CI/CD pipeline not explicitly described beyond GitHub branch rules
No SSO mentioned for customer authentication
Cybersecurity insurance control could not be tested (no incidents during period, but also very short period)
Several controls untestable due to no events occurring in the short audit window
No caching layer mentioned despite being an AI platform with likely high-throughput needs
Render listed as infrastructure provider in company metadata but barely appears in the SOC 2 report itself
green_flags: Clean unqualified opinion with zero exceptions across all tested controls
MFA enforced for administrative access, remote access, and source code access
Strong change management: branch protection, code review required, separate environments, emergency change process
Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party
Monthly vulnerability scanning with remediation tracking
Formal vendor management program with annual SOC report reviews and quarterly assessments
Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration and alerting
BCDR plans tested annually with different loss scenarios
Quarterly user access reviews for all accounts including administrative privileges
Access revoked within one business day upon termination
Data classification policy established and implemented
Board of Directors with Risk and Governance Executive Committee (RGEC) providing oversight
IT Leadership Committee meeting weekly to review security activities
key_observations: Network diagram reveals actual tech stack (Supabase, Vercel, GitHub, Sentry, Google Gemini, Linear) that is not described in text - the diagram is the most informative technical artifact in the entire report

The report lists Render as infrastructure provider in metadata but the architecture diagram shows Supabase as the primary backend; Vercel handles deployment. Render's role is unclear from the report itself.
For an 'AI-first platform,' there is remarkably little disclosure about AI/ML infrastructure, model hosting, prompt security, or AI-specific controls
The company appears to be very early stage - only CEO and CTO roles documented, with the CTO (Samarth Lamba) serving as signing authority
Google Gemini appears in the network diagram as the AI backend, creating a single-provider dependency for the company's core AI capabilities
The system description is almost entirely generic boilerplate language common to SOC 2 report templates - very little Lumari-specific technical detail
Despite excluding Privacy as a trust criteria, the report mentions data classification policies and data retention - suggesting some privacy controls exist but were not formally tested

The carve-out method for Supabase means a significant portion of the security controls relevant to a tech due diligence are not covered in this report and would need to be evaluated via Supabase's own SOC 2 report
